title: Jump Game
description: Determine if you can reach last index of array

examples.explanation: Start at index 0 (value 2). From index 0, try jump 1 to index 1: At index 1 (value 3). From index 1, try jump 1 to index 2: At index 2 (value 1). From index 2, try jump 1 to index 3: At index 3 (value 1). From index 3, try jump 1 to index 4: Reached last index (4). Return true. (Other paths exist but we found success).

Start at index 0 (value 3). From index 0, try jump 1 to index 1: At index 1 (value 2). From index 1, try jump 1 to index 2: At index 2 (value 1). From index 2, try jump 1 to index 3: At index 3 (value 0). Cannot jump from index 3 (0 value). Backtrack to index 1, try jump 2 to index 3: Same dead end. Backtrack to index 0, try jump 2 to index 2: At index 2 (value 1). From index 2, jump to index 3: Dead end. Backtrack to index 0, try jump 3 to index 3: Dead end. All paths exhausted, return false.

approaches.name: Brute Force Backtracking O(2^n)
approaches.summary: Recursively try all possible jumps from each index, exploring every path to see if any reaches the last index. This is a naive approach that checks all combinations but is impractical for large arrays due to exponential time.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Backtracking for Jump Game
ladders.desc: Complete recursive solution that explores all possible jump paths to determine if the last index is reachable
ladders.explanation: The brute force backtracking approach solves the Jump Game problem by systematically exploring every possible path through the array. The intuition is that from any position i, we can jump to any index from i+1 to i+nums[i] (within array bounds). By recursively trying all these possibilities, we can determine if there exists at least one path that reaches the last index. This is essentially a depth-first search through the state space of possible positions. The algorithm starts at index 0 and recursively explores all reachable indices from the current position. For each recursive call, we check if we've reached the last index (base case success) or if we're at a position with 0 jump value (which would be a dead end unless we're already at the last index). The time complexity is O(2^n) because in the worst case (like nums = [n-1, n-2, ..., 1, 0, 0]), each position can jump to almost all subsequent positions, creating an exponential number of recursive calls. Space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack in the worst case. This approach connects to L1 by implementing the complete recursive backtracking function that calls the helper from L1. Common mistakes include not handling the base case properly (forgetting to check if current position equals last index), not bounding the jump range correctly, and creating infinite recursion by not progressing forward. The real code pattern involves a recursive function that iterates through all possible jumps from the current position.

examples.trace: Start at index 0 (value 2). From index 0, try jump 1 to index 1: At index 1 (value 3). From index 1, try jump 1 to index 2: At index 2 (value 1). From index 2, try jump 1 to index 3: At index 3 (value 1). From index 3, try jump 1 to index 4: Reached last index (4). Return true. (Other paths exist but we found success).

Start at index 0 (value 3). From index 0, try jump 1 to index 1: At index 1 (value 2). From index 1, try jump 1 to index 2: At index 2 (value 1). From index 2, try jump 1 to index 3: At index 3 (value 0). Cannot jump from index 3 (0 value). Backtrack to index 1, try jump 2 to index 3: Same dead end. Backtrack to index 0, try jump 2 to index 2: At index 2 (value 1). From index 2, jump to index 3: Dead end. Backtrack to index 0, try jump 3 to index 3: Dead end. All paths exhausted, return false.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Jump Explorer Function
ladders.desc: The recursive function that explores all possible jumps from a given position
ladders.explanation: This recursive function is the heart of the brute force backtracking approach. The intuition is that from any position 'pos' in the array, we need to determine if we can reach the last index by making jumps allowed by nums[pos]. The function works by first checking if we've already reached or passed the last index (base case). If not, it calculates the maximum jump distance from the current position (min of nums[pos] and remaining distance to avoid out-of-bounds). Then it recursively calls itself for each possible jump length from 1 to maxJump. If any recursive call returns true, we propagate true upward; otherwise, we return false after exhausting all possibilities. The time complexity for this function alone is O(b^d) where b is the average branching factor and d is the depth, but in context of the full problem it contributes to the O(2^n) complexity. Space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack. This connects to L0 as the main recursive engine, and to L2 as it implements the backtracking logic. Common mistakes include incorrect base case handling (checking pos == n-1 instead of pos >= n-1), not limiting jumps to array bounds, and using the wrong loop range for jump distances. The real code pattern is a function that takes current position and array, returns boolean, with recursive calls for each possible jump.

examples.trace: canJumpFrom(0, [2,3,1,1,4]): pos=0 < 4 (last index). maxJump = min(2, 4-0) = 2. Try jump=1: canJumpFrom(1, array) -> pos=1 < 4, maxJump=min(3,3)=3, try jump=1: canJumpFrom(2, array) -> pos=2 < 4, maxJump=min(1,2)=1, try jump=1: canJumpFrom(3, array) -> pos=3 < 4, maxJump=min(1,1)=1, try jump=1: canJumpFrom(4, array) -> pos=4 >= 4, return true. Propagate true back up.

canJumpFrom(2, [3,2,1,0,4]): pos=2 < 4. maxJump = min(1, 4-2) = 1. Try jump=1: canJumpFrom(3, array) -> pos=3 < 4, maxJump = min(0, 1) = 0, no jumps possible, return false. Only one jump tried, all false, return false.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Backtracking Through Jump Possibilities
ladders.desc: The backtracking technique of trying all jump distances from a position
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the backtracking logic itself: from a given position with jump value k, we need to try jumping 1 step, 2 steps, ..., up to k steps (or until we'd go out of bounds). The intuition is that we must explore all possibilities because we don't know which jump length will lead to success. This is a classic exhaustive search pattern. The algorithm involves: 1) Determine maximum possible jump (min of nums[pos] and distance to end). 2) For jump distance from 1 to maxJump: make the jump to new position = pos + jump, then recursively explore from there. 3) If any jump leads to success, return true immediately (optimization). 4) If all jumps fail, return false. The time complexity for this logic at one level is O(k) where k is the jump value, but when combined recursively it creates exponential complexity. Space complexity is O(1) for this logic itself (not counting recursion). This connects to L1 as the loop inside the recursive function, and to L3 as it uses array value access and position updating. Common mistakes include starting loop from 0 instead of 1 (jumping 0 steps causes infinite recursion), not limiting to array bounds, and not handling the case where maxJump is 0. The real code pattern is a for loop from 1 to maxJump, with recursive call for each iteration.

examples.input: Position: 1 with value 3 in array of length 5
examples.trace: At position 1, value = 3. Distance to end = 5-1-1 = 3 (indices: 1 to 4). maxJump = min(3, 3) = 3. Try jump=1: new position = 2. Explore from position 2. Try jump=2: new position = 3. Explore from position 3. Try jump=3: new position = 4. Explore from position 4 (last index).

examples.input: Position: 3 with value 0 in array of length 5
examples.trace: At position 3, value = 0. Distance to end = 5-3-1 = 1. maxJump = min(0, 1) = 0. No jumps to try (loop from 1 to 0 doesn't execute). Return false.

ladders.title: Building Block — Determining Maximum Jump from a Position
ladders.desc: Calculating how far you can jump from a given position without going out of bounds
ladders.explanation: This building block addresses a critical sub-problem: given a position 'pos' in an array of length n, and a jump value nums[pos], determine the maximum number of steps you can actually jump without exceeding array bounds. The intuition is that even if nums[pos] is large, you cannot jump beyond the last index (n-1). The algorithm is simple: maxJump = min(nums[pos], n-1-pos). Here, n-1-pos represents the maximum possible jump that would land exactly at the last index. For example, if you're at position 2 in a 5-element array (indices 0-4), the maximum useful jump is to position 4, which requires 2 steps (4-2). If nums[2] = 5, you can only use 2 of those 5. The time complexity is O(1), space complexity is O(1). This connects to L2 as it provides the upper bound for the backtracking loop, and to L4 as it uses array indexing and arithmetic. Common mistakes include using n instead of n-1 (off-by-one), forgetting to subtract current position, or misunderstanding that we want to reach index n-1, not go beyond it. The real code pattern is a simple min() calculation.

examples.trace: n = 5, last index = 4. Current position = 0. Distance to last index = 4 - 0 = 4. nums[0] = 4. min(4, 4) = 4. Can jump up to 4 steps.
n = 6, last index = 5. Current position = 3. Distance to last index = 5 - 3 = 2. nums[3] = 5. min(5, 2) = 2. Can only jump up to 2 steps (to positions 4 or 5).

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing Array Value and Updating Position
ladders.desc: Fundamental operations of reading array values and calculating new positions
ladders.explanation: This level covers the most basic operations needed for the jump game: accessing an array element by index and performing arithmetic to calculate a new position. The intuition is that nums[i] tells us how far we can jump from position i, and i + jump gives us the new position after jumping. The algorithm involves: 1) Access nums[pos] to get jump capacity. 2) Choose a jump distance j (where 1 ≤ j ≤ nums[pos], and within bounds). 3) Calculate new position = pos + j. These are O(1) operations for both time and space. This connects to L3 as these operations are used in determining maximum jump and making jumps, and to L5 as it builds on array fundamentals. Common mistakes include using 0-based vs 1-based indexing confusion, not validating that new position is within array bounds, or misunderstanding that nums[i] represents maximum jump, not exact jump. The real code pattern is simple array indexing and addition.

examples.trace: Access nums[0] = 2. Choose jump distance = 1 (valid since 1 ≤ 2). New position = 0 + 1 = 1. Verify 1 < array length 3.

Access nums[2] = 2. Choose jump distance = 1 (valid since 1 ≤ 2). New position = 2 + 1 = 3. But array length = 3, valid indices are 0-2. Position 3 is out of bounds - this would need to be caught by bounds checking.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Bounds
ladders.desc: Understanding array indices, zero-based indexing, and valid index ranges
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept covers the basics of array indexing that are prerequisite for the Jump Game problem. The intuition is that arrays are ordered collections where each element has a position called an index. In most programming languages, array indices start at 0 (zero-based indexing). For an array of length n, valid indices are 0, 1, 2, ..., n-1. Trying to access an index outside this range causes an error (index out of bounds). The algorithm for working with arrays always involves: 1) Knowing the array length n. 2) Ensuring any index i satisfies 0 ≤ i < n. 3) Accessing element at index i using syntax like nums[i]. Time and space complexity for indexing is O(1). This connects to L4 as all array operations depend on correct indexing. Common mistakes include thinking indices start at 1, using i ≤ n instead of i < n, or forgetting that the last index is n-1 not n. The real code pattern is using indices within the valid range.

examples.trace: Array has 3 elements. Index 0 = 10, index 1 = 20, index 2 = 30. Index 3 is invalid (3 ≥ 3). Index -1 is invalid (-1 < 0).
Array has 1 element. Index 0 = 5. Index 1 is invalid (1 ≥ 1). Index 0 is the only valid index.

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming with Memoization O(n^2)
approaches.summary: Use memoization to store whether each index can reach the last index, reducing redundant calculations from brute force. This approach iterates from the end to the start, marking reachable indices based on jump values.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Jump Game with Memoization
ladders.desc: Complete solution using DP memoization to determine if last index is reachable
ladders.explanation: This is the complete Jump Game problem solved using dynamic programming with memoization. The intuition is to work backwards from the last index, marking each position as reachable if it can jump to any already-reachable position. We create a memo array where memo[i] stores whether index i can reach the last index. Starting from the second-to-last index moving backward, for each position i, we check if i + nums[i] >= last index (direct reach) OR if there exists any j where i < j <= i + nums[i] AND memo[j] is True (indirect reach through intermediate positions). This avoids redundant calculations from brute force recursion. The algorithm: 1. Initialize memo array of size n with all False. 2. Set memo[n-1] = True (last index is reachable). 3. For i from n-2 down to 0: a. If i + nums[i] >= n-1, set memo[i] = True. b. Else, for j from i+1 to min(i+nums[i], n-1): if memo[j] is True, set memo[i] = True and break. 4. Return memo[0]. Time complexity is O(n²) worst-case when each position can jump to many others. Space is O(n) for memo array. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete reachability checking logic. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loop bounds and forgetting to handle direct jumps. Real code pattern: def canJump(nums): n=len(nums); memo=[False]*n; memo[-1]=True; for i in range(n-2,-1,-1): max_jump=min(i+nums[i],n-1); if max_jump>=n-1: memo[i]=True; else: for j in range(i+1,max_jump+1): if memo[j]: memo[i]=True; break; return memo[0]

examples.trace: nums=[2,3,1,1,4], n=5. Initialize memo=[F,F,F,F,F]. Set memo[4]=True (last index). i=3: nums[3]=1, i+nums[3]=4 >=4, so memo[3]=True. i=2: nums[2]=1, i+nums[2]=3, check j=3: memo[3]=True, so memo[2]=True. i=1: nums[1]=3, i+nums[1]=4 >=4, so memo[1]=True. i=0: nums[0]=2, i+nums[0]=2, check j=1: memo[1]=True, so memo[0]=True. Return memo[0]=True.

nums=[3,2,1,0,4], n=5. Initialize memo=[F,F,F,F,F]. Set memo[4]=True. i=3: nums[3]=0, i+nums[3]=3, check j=4: j>3 so no check, memo[3]=False. i=2: nums[2]=1, i+nums[2]=3, check j=3: memo[3]=False, memo[2]=False. i=1: nums[1]=2, i+nums[1]=3, check j=2: memo[2]=False, j=3: memo[3]=False, memo[1]=False. i=0: nums[0]=3, i+nums[0]=3, check j=1: memo[1]=False, j=2: memo[2]=False, j=3: memo[3]=False, memo[0]=False. Return memo[0]=False.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Reachability Check for Single Index
ladders.desc: Determine if a specific index can reach the last index given memo of later indices
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine checks whether a given index i can reach the last index, assuming we already know which later indices can reach the end (via memo array). The intuition is that position i is reachable if it can either jump directly to the last index OR jump to any intermediate position j that is already known to be reachable. This encapsulates the core decision logic of the DP approach. Algorithm: Given nums array, index i, memo array (for indices > i), and n: 1. Calculate max_reach = min(i + nums[i], n-1). 2. If max_reach >= n-1, return True (direct jump). 3. Else, for j from i+1 to max_reach: if memo[j] is True, return True. 4. Return False. Time complexity is O(k) where k = nums[i] (bounded by n), space O(1). This feeds into L0 by being the logic executed for each i in the backward loop. Common mistakes include not using min() to avoid index out of bounds and incorrect loop start (should be i+1, not i). Real code pattern: def canReachFrom(i, nums, memo): n=len(nums); max_jump=min(i+nums[i], n-1); if max_jump>=n-1: return True; for j in range(i+1, max_jump+1): if memo[j]: return True; return False

examples.trace: n=5, i=2, nums[2]=1. max_jump=min(2+1,4)=3. max_jump=3 <4, so check indirect jumps. j=3: memo[3]=True, so return True.
n=5, i=1, nums[1]=2. max_jump=min(1+2,4)=3. max_jump=3 <4, check indirect jumps. j=2: memo[2]=False, j=3: memo[3]=False. No True found, return False.

testCases.expected: Error (i out of bounds for n=1)

ladders.title: Core Logic — Backward DP with Memoization
ladders.desc: The technique of solving from end to start using stored results
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the core DP technique: solving the problem backward while memoizing results to avoid recomputation. The intuition is that by starting from the last index (which is trivially reachable) and moving backward, we can use previously computed results for later indices to determine reachability of earlier indices. This transforms an exponential brute-force problem into polynomial time. The key insight is optimal substructure: whether i can reach the end depends only on whether positions reachable from i can reach the end. Algorithm outline: 1. Create memo array of size n initialized False. 2. Base case: memo[n-1] = True. 3. For i from n-2 down to 0: set memo[i] using reachability check (from L1). 4. Answer is memo[0]. Time O(n²), space O(n). This connects to L1 by providing the memo array that the reachability check uses. Common mistakes include incorrect iteration direction (should be backward) and not initializing the base case. Real code pattern: def canJump(nums): n=len(nums); memo=[False]*n; memo[-1]=True; for i in range(n-2, -1, -1): # implement reachability check here; return memo[0]

examples.trace: n=3, memo=[F,F,F]. Base: memo[2]=True. i=1: nums[1]=0, max_jump=1, check j=2: memo[2]=True, so memo[1]=True. i=0: nums[0]=2, max_jump=2, check j=1: memo[1]=True, so memo[0]=True. Result True.

n=4, memo=[F,F,F,F]. Base: memo[3]=True. i=2: nums[2]=1, max_jump=3, check j=3: memo[3]=True, so memo[2]=True. i=1: nums[1]=0, max_jump=1, check j=2: memo[2]=True, so memo[1]=True. i=0: nums[0]=1, max_jump=1, check j=1: memo[1]=True, so memo[0]=True. Wait—this gives True, but correct is False! Error: i=1 check is wrong because max_jump=min(1+0,3)=1, but j=2 >1, so shouldn't check j=2. Corrected: i=1: max_jump=1, j from 2 to 1 has no iterations, so memo[1]=False. Then i=0: max_jump=1, check j=1: memo[1]=False, so memo[0]=False. Result False.

ladders.title: Building Block — Memoization Array Initialization and Update
ladders.desc: Creating and updating a DP memo array for reachability
ladders.explanation: This level covers the foundational concept of memoization in DP: using an array to store computed results for subproblems. The intuition is to avoid redundant work by remembering answers to overlapping subproblems. For Jump Game, the subproblem is 'can index i reach the last index?' We store this in memo[i]. The process involves: 1. Initialization: Create array of size n with default values (False, meaning unknown/unreachable). 2. Base case: Set memo[n-1] = True because the last index is trivially reachable. 3. Update rule: For each i from n-2 to 0, set memo[i] based on nums[i] and memo values of indices i+1 to i+nums[i]. This is a bottom-up DP approach. Time O(n) for initialization and base case, space O(n). This connects to L2 by providing the memo array structure that enables backward DP. Common mistakes include incorrect array size or not setting the base case. Real code pattern: n=len(nums); memo=[False]*n; memo[-1]=True; # then fill memo backward

examples.trace: n=5. Create memo array of 5 elements: [False,False,False,False,False]. Set memo[4]=True (base case). Result: [False,False,False,False,True].

examples.input: nums=[3,2,1,0,4], update memo for i=2 assuming memo=[F,F,F,False,True]
examples.trace: Given memo=[False,False,False,False,True] after base case. For i=2: nums[2]=1, max_jump=min(2+1,4)=3. Check j=3: memo[3]=False. No other j, so memo[2] remains False. Updated memo: [False,False,False,False,True].

testCases.input: nums=[2,0,0], update memo for i=1 with memo=[F,False,True]
nums=[1,0,1,0], update memo for i=0 with memo=[False,False,True,True]
nums=[3,0,8,2,0,0,1], update memo for i=3 with memo=[F,F,F,False,False,False,True]
nums=[1,2,3,4,5], update memo for i=4 (invalid, already base)
nums=[0,2,3], update memo for i=0 with memo=[False,False,True]
nums=[1,1,1,1,1], update memo for i=3 with memo=[F,F,F,False,True]
nums=[2,3,1,1,4], update memo for i=3 with memo=[F,F,F,False,True]

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Checking Jump Reachability Range
ladders.desc: Determining which indices can be reached from a given position
ladders.explanation: This level involves the basic operation of calculating the range of indices that can be reached from a current index i given nums[i]. The intuition is simple: from position i, you can jump to any index from i+1 to i+nums[i], inclusive, but not beyond the array bounds. This is fundamental because it defines the possible next steps. The operation requires: 1. Compute max_reach = i + nums[i]. 2. Clamp max_reach to n-1 using min() to avoid index errors. 3. The reachable range is indices i+1 to max_reach. If max_reach >= n-1, it means you can jump directly to the last index. Time O(1), space O(1). This connects to L3 by being used in the update rule for memo[i]. Common mistakes include forgetting to clamp or including i itself in the range. Real code pattern: max_jump=min(i+nums[i], n-1); if max_jump >= n-1: direct reach; else check indices i+1 to max_jump

examples.trace: i=0, nums[0]=2. max_reach=0+2=2. n-1=4. min(2,4)=2. max_jump=2. Since 2<4, no direct reach. Reachable indices: from i+1=1 to max_jump=2, so indices 1 and 2.
i=3, nums[3]=5. max_reach=3+5=8. n-1=4. min(8,4)=4. max_jump=4. Since max_jump=4 >= n-1=4, direct reach to last index is possible.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Looping Backward
ladders.desc: Basic operations of accessing array elements and iterating in reverse
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: working with arrays by indexing and looping backward. The intuition is that arrays store elements accessible by indices (0-based), and we can traverse them in reverse order to solve problems from the end. Key operations: 1. Access element at index i: nums[i]. 2. Get array length: n = len(nums). 3. Last index: n-1. 4. Loop backward: for i in range(start, stop, step) with step=-1, e.g., range(n-1, -1, -1) goes from last to first. This is foundational because the DP approach requires checking array values and iterating from the end. Time O(1) per access, O(n) for full traversal. Space O(1). This connects to L4 by providing the mechanics to implement reachability checks. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loop bounds or confusing indices. Real code pattern: n=len(nums); for i in range(n-1, -1, -1): # use nums[i]

examples.input: nums=[2,3,1,1,4], loop backward and print indices
examples.trace: n=5. Loop: i=4, print 4; i=3, print 3; i=2, print 2; i=1, print 1; i=0, print 0.
n=1. Loop: i=0, access nums[0]=0.

testCases.input: nums=[1,1,1,1,1], loop from n-2 to 0 indices

approaches.name: Greedy Backward Iteration O(n)
approaches.summary: Iterate from the second-last index to the start, updating a target index that must be reachable. If an index can jump to or beyond the target, it becomes the new target; check if the start index reaches the target.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Jump Game with Greedy Backward Iteration
ladders.desc: Complete solution to determine if you can reach the last index using backward iteration with target tracking.
ladders.explanation: The greedy backward iteration approach solves the Jump Game problem by working from the end of the array toward the beginning, maintaining a target index that must be reachable. The intuition is that if we can reach the target from an earlier index, we can effectively move the target backward. Starting with the last index as the target, we iterate from the second-last index to the first. At each position i, we check if i + nums[i] >= target. If true, it means we can jump from i to or beyond the current target, so we update target to i. After the loop, if target equals 0, the start index can reach the end, returning true; otherwise, false. This works because we're essentially verifying a chain of reachability backward: if we can reach the target from i, then reaching i becomes the new requirement. The algorithm runs in O(n) time as it processes each element once, and O(1) space since it only uses a few variables. It connects to the overall problem by providing an optimal solution that avoids recursion or dynamic programming overhead. Common mistakes include incorrect loop bounds (e.g., starting from the last index instead of second-last) or misplacing the update condition. Real code pattern: def canJump(nums): target = len(nums)-1; for i in range(len(nums)-2, -1, -1): if i + nums[i] >= target: target = i; return target == 0.

examples.trace: Initialize target = 4 (last index). Iterate backward: i=3, nums[3]=1, 3+1=4 >= target=4, update target=3. i=2, nums[2]=1, 2+1=3 >= target=3, update target=2. i=1, nums[1]=3, 1+3=4 >= target=2, update target=1. i=0, nums[0]=2, 0+2=2 >= target=1, update target=0. Loop ends, target=0, return true.

Initialize target = 4. Iterate backward: i=3, nums[3]=0, 3+0=3 < target=4, target remains 4. i=2, nums[2]=1, 2+1=3 < target=4, target remains 4. i=1, nums[1]=2, 1+2=3 < target=4, target remains 4. i=0, nums[0]=3, 0+3=3 < target=4, target remains 4. Loop ends, target=4 != 0, return false.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Backward Reachability Check
ladders.desc: Implement the core loop that updates the target index based on jump reachability.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine focuses on the iterative process that determines if earlier indices can reach a moving target. The intuition is to simulate backward propagation of reachability: if an index can jump to or beyond the current target, it becomes the new target, meaning we only need to reach that index instead. Start with target set to the last index. Then, for each index i from second-last down to first, check if i + nums[i] >= target. If true, update target to i. This reduces the problem to checking if we can eventually move the target to the start. The algorithm runs in O(n) time for the loop and O(1) space. It connects to L0 by being the main logic that solves the full problem after initialization. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loop indices or incorrect comparison (using > instead of >=). Real code pattern: target = len(nums)-1; for i in range(len(nums)-2, -1, -1): if i + nums[i] >= target: target = i.

examples.trace: Start target=4. i=3: 3+1=4 >=4, target=3. i=2: 2+1=3 >=3, target=2. i=1: 1+3=4 >=2, target=1. i=0: 0+2=2 >=1, target=0. Final target=0.
Start target=2. i=1: 1+0=1 <2, target stays 2. i=0: 0+1=1 <2, target stays 2. Final target=2.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Greedy Target Update Condition
ladders.desc: Understand and apply the condition i + nums[i] >= target to update the target index.
ladders.explanation: This level delves into the central insight of the greedy approach: the condition i + nums[i] >= target determines if an index i can make the target reachable. The intuition is that nums[i] represents the maximum jump length from i, so i + nums[i] is the farthest index reachable from i. If this is at least the current target, then i can jump to or beyond target, making i the new point we need to reach. This is greedy because we always update target to the leftmost index that can reach it, optimizing for earliest reachability. The algorithm involves checking this condition for each i in reverse order. Time is O(1) per check, space O(1). It connects to L1 as the predicate used in the loop. Common mistakes include misunderstanding the inequality (using > instead of >=) or miscalculating i + nums[i]. Real code pattern: if i + nums[i] >= target: target = i.

examples.trace: Compute i + nums[i] = 3 + 1 = 4. Check 4 >= 4: true, so update target from 4 to 3.
Compute i + nums[i] = 1 + 0 = 1. Check 1 >= 2: false, so target remains 2.

ladders.title: Building Block — Reverse Array Iteration
ladders.desc: Iterate through an array from the end to the beginning, accessing elements and indices.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves traversing an array in reverse order, which is essential for the greedy backward approach. The intuition is that by starting from the end, we can build solution information backward, similar to dynamic programming but optimized. To iterate reverse, use a loop that decrements an index from the last index down to 0. For an array of length n, indices go from 0 to n-1, so reverse iteration goes from n-1 to 0. This allows processing elements in opposite order to forward iteration. Time is O(n) for full traversal, space O(1). It connects to L2 by providing the framework to apply the target update condition in reverse. Common mistakes include incorrect start/end indices (e.g., starting at n instead of n-1) or using positive increments. Real code pattern: for i in range(len(arr)-1, -1, -1): access arr[i].

examples.trace: Length n=4, indices 0 to 3. Reverse loop i from 3 to 0: i=3 arr[3]=40, i=2 arr[2]=30, i=1 arr[1]=20, i=0 arr[0]=10.
Length n=1, indices 0 to 0. Reverse loop i from 0 to 0: i=0 arr[0]=5.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Index Arithmetic with Array Values
ladders.desc: Compute expressions like i + nums[i] using array indices and values.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves accessing an array element at index i and performing arithmetic with the index itself. In the Jump Game, nums[i] is the maximum jump length from i, so i + nums[i] gives the farthest reachable index from i. The intuition is that array indices represent positions, and their values represent jump capabilities; combining them calculates potential destinations. To perform this, first access nums[i] using bracket notation, then add i. This requires careful handling of indices to avoid out-of-bounds errors, but i + nums[i] can exceed array bounds, which is acceptable in this context. Time and space are O(1). It connects to L3 as a computation done during reverse iteration. Common mistakes include using the value alone without adding i or misindexing. Real code pattern: reach = i + nums[i].

examples.trace: Access nums[2] = 4. Compute i + nums[2] = 2 + 4 = 6.
Access nums[0] = 0. Compute i + nums[0] = 0 + 0 = 0.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Element Access
ladders.desc: Access elements in an array using indices, understanding zero-based indexing.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: working with arrays by accessing elements via their indices. An array is a collection of items stored at contiguous memory locations, and each item can be accessed using an index starting from 0. For example, in an array nums = [10, 20, 30], nums[0] gives 10, nums[1] gives 20, and nums[2] gives 30. The intuition is that indices act as positions or addresses within the array. To access an element, use bracket notation with the index, ensuring it is within the valid range (0 to length-1). This operation is O(1) time and space. It connects to L4 as the basic step to get nums[i] for arithmetic. Common mistakes include using 1-based indexing or accessing out-of-bounds indices. Real code pattern: element = nums[index].

examples.trace: Array nums has length 3, indices 0,1,2. Access nums[1]: the element at position 1 is 8.
Array nums has length 1, indices 0. Access nums[0]: the element at position 0 is 100.

approaches.name: Greedy Forward Max Reach O(n)
approaches.summary: Iterate from the start, maintaining the maximum index reachable so far. If at any point the current index exceeds the max reach, return false; otherwise, update max reach and check if it reaches the last index.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Jump Game with Greedy Forward Max Reach
ladders.desc: Complete solution to determine if you can reach the last index using greedy forward max reach approach.
ladders.explanation: The Jump Game problem asks whether you can reach the last index of an array where each element represents the maximum jump length from that position. The greedy forward max reach approach solves this optimally in O(n) time and O(1) space by maintaining the furthest index reachable as we iterate through the array. The intuition is that we don't need to track all possible paths—we only care about the maximum reachable index at each step. If we can reach or exceed the last index, we succeed. If at any point our current position exceeds our maximum reach, we fail because we can't progress further. This works because the maximum reach represents the best possible progress from all positions we've visited so far. The algorithm: 1. Initialize max_reach = 0 (the furthest index we can reach). 2. Iterate i from 0 to n-1: a. If i > max_reach, return False (we're stuck). b. Update max_reach = max(max_reach, i + nums[i]). c. If max_reach >= n-1, return True. 3. Return False if loop completes without reaching last index. Time complexity is O(n) since we traverse the array once. Space complexity is O(1) using only a few variables. Connection to L1: This integrates the max reach update logic with the iteration framework. Common mistakes include: starting max_reach at nums[0] instead of 0 (fails for empty arrays), checking i > max_reach after updating max_reach (incorrect logic), or using min/max incorrectly. Real code pattern: def canJump(nums): max_reach = 0; for i, jump in enumerate(nums): if i > max_reach: return False; max_reach = max(max_reach, i + jump); if max_reach >= len(nums)-1: return True; return False.

examples.trace: Start: max_reach = 0, n = 5, last index = 4. i=0: i=0 ≤ max_reach=0 → continue. Update max_reach = max(0, 0+2) = 2. max_reach=2 < 4 → continue. i=1: i=1 ≤ max_reach=2 → continue. Update max_reach = max(2, 1+3) = 4. max_reach=4 ≥ 4 → return true.

Start: max_reach = 0, n = 5, last index = 4. i=0: i=0 ≤ max_reach=0 → continue. Update max_reach = max(0, 0+3) = 3. max_reach=3 < 4 → continue. i=1: i=1 ≤ max_reach=3 → continue. Update max_reach = max(3, 1+2) = 3. max_reach=3 < 4 → continue. i=2: i=2 ≤ max_reach=3 → continue. Update max_reach = max(3, 2+1) = 3. max_reach=3 < 4 → continue. i=3: i=3 ≤ max_reach=3 → continue. Update max_reach = max(3, 3+0) = 3. max_reach=3 < 4 → continue. i=4: i=4 > max_reach=3 → return false.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Update Maximum Reachable Index
ladders.desc: The core function that updates the maximum index reachable based on current position and jump value.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the greedy approach: given a current index i and a jump value nums[i], it computes the furthest index we can reach from i and updates the global maximum reach. The intuition is that at each step, we want to know the best possible progress we can make from all positions visited so far. By keeping track of the maximum reach, we avoid exploring all paths and focus only on the optimal extension. The algorithm: 1. Input: current index i, jump value jump, current max_reach. 2. Compute potential_reach = i + jump. 3. Update max_reach = max(max_reach, potential_reach). 4. Return updated max_reach. Time complexity is O(1) per update. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L0: This update is performed at each iteration in the full solution. Connection to L2: This implements the greedy choice of taking the maximum reach. Common mistakes include: forgetting to add i to jump (computing just jump), using min instead of max, or not initializing max_reach properly. Real code pattern: def update_max_reach(max_reach, i, jump): return max(max_reach, i + jump). This sub-problem can be coded independently by testing with various inputs to ensure correct updates.

examples.trace: Start: max_reach=2, i=1, jump=3. Compute potential_reach = i + jump = 1 + 3 = 4. Update max_reach = max(2, 4) = 4. Return 4.
Start: max_reach=5, i=3, jump=0. Compute potential_reach = i + jump = 3 + 0 = 3. Update max_reach = max(5, 3) = 5. Return 5.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Greedy Choice of Maximum Reach
ladders.desc: The central insight of always extending the maximum reachable index greedily.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the greedy strategy: at each step, we extend our reach as far as possible based on current information. The intuition is that in the Jump Game, the optimal way to reach the end is to always maximize how far we can go from any position we can access. By maintaining the maximum reach, we ensure we never miss a better opportunity from an earlier index. This works because if we can reach index j, we can also reach any index ≤ j, so focusing on the maximum is sufficient. The algorithm: 1. Start with max_reach = 0 (we can reach index 0). 2. For each index i we can reach (i ≤ max_reach), consider the jump nums[i] and update max_reach to the maximum of its current value and i + nums[i]. 3. Continue until we either reach the last index or exhaust reachable indices. Time complexity depends on iteration but is O(n) in full context. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L1: This logic uses the update function repeatedly. Connection to L3: It relies on iterating through indices. Common mistakes include: updating max_reach before checking if i is reachable (leading to incorrect jumps), or not handling the case where max_reach doesn't increase. Real code pattern: A loop that updates max_reach greedily: for i in range(n): if i <= max_reach: max_reach = max(max_reach, i + nums[i]). This sub-problem can be practiced by simulating the greedy updates on paper.

examples.trace: Start: max_reach=0. i=0: i=0 ≤ max_reach=0 → update max_reach = max(0, 0+2)=2. i=1: i=1 ≤ max_reach=2 → update max_reach = max(2, 1+3)=4. i=2: i=2 ≤ max_reach=4 → update max_reach = max(4, 2+1)=4. i=3: i=3 ≤ max_reach=4 → update max_reach = max(4, 3+1)=4. i=4: i=4 ≤ max_reach=4 → update max_reach = max(4, 4+4)=8. Final max_reach=8, but we stop early if max_reach≥4.

Start: max_reach=0. i=0: i=0 ≤ max_reach=0 → update max_reach = max(0, 0+1)=1. i=1: i=1 ≤ max_reach=1 → update max_reach = max(1, 1+0)=1. i=2: i=2 > max_reach=1 → cannot update because i is not reachable. Final max_reach=1.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating with Reachability Check
ladders.desc: Iterating through array indices while checking if current index is reachable based on max reach.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves traversing the array and at each step, determining if the current index i is within the maximum reachable index max_reach. The intuition is that we can only use the jump at index i if we have actually reached that index through previous jumps. The check i ≤ max_reach ensures we don't consider unreachable positions. This is crucial because the greedy approach assumes we only update from indices we can access. The algorithm: 1. Initialize max_reach = 0. 2. For i from 0 to n-1: a. Check if i ≤ max_reach. b. If true, we can proceed (e.g., update max_reach). c. If false, we cannot proceed further (in full problem, return false). 3. Continue until end of array or early exit. Time complexity is O(n) for the iteration. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L2: This iteration framework enables the greedy updates. Connection to L4: It uses comparison operations. Common mistakes include: using strict inequality i < max_reach (missing equality case where i is exactly reachable), or checking reachability after updating max_reach. Real code pattern: for i in range(len(nums)): if i <= max_reach: # do something. This sub-problem can be coded as a function that returns whether all indices are reachable given a max_reach progression.

examples.output: Reachability: i=0 reachable, i=1 reachable, i=2 reachable, i=3 reachable, i=4 reachable
examples.trace: Start: max_reach values correspond after each update. Check each i: i=0 ≤ max_reach=0 → reachable. i=1 ≤ max_reach=2 → reachable. i=2 ≤ max_reach=4 → reachable. i=3 ≤ max_reach=4 → reachable. i=4 ≤ max_reach=4 → reachable.

examples.output: Reachability: i=0 reachable, i=1 reachable, i=2 reachable, i=3 reachable, i=4 not reachable
examples.trace: Start: max_reach values after updates. Check each i: i=0 ≤ max_reach=0 → reachable. i=1 ≤ max_reach=3 → reachable. i=2 ≤ max_reach=3 → reachable. i=3 ≤ max_reach=3 → reachable. i=4 > max_reach=3 → not reachable.

testCases.expected: Reachability: i=0 reachable, i=1 reachable, i=2 reachable
Reachability: i=0 reachable, i=1 not reachable
Reachability: i=0 reachable, i=1 reachable, i=2 reachable
Reachability: i=0 reachable, i=1 reachable, i=2 not reachable
Reachability: i=0 reachable, i=1 reachable, i=2 reachable, i=3 reachable
Reachability: i=0 reachable, i=1 not reachable, i=2 not reachable
Reachability: i=0 reachable, i=1 reachable, i=2 reachable, i=3 reachable, i=4 reachable
Reachability: i=0 reachable, i=1 reachable, i=2 reachable, i=3 reachable
Reachability: i=0 reachable, i=1 reachable, i=2 reachable

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Index and Jump Value Addition
ladders.desc: Fundamental operation of adding an index to its jump value to compute reachable index.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation computes how far we can jump from a given position: reachable_index = current_index + jump_value. The intuition is simple: if you are at index i and can jump up to nums[i] steps, the furthest index you can reach from i is i + nums[i]. This is a key arithmetic step in the greedy approach. The algorithm: 1. Input: current index i (an integer), jump value jump (an integer from nums[i]). 2. Output: i + jump. 3. This represents the maximum index reachable in one jump from i. Time complexity is O(1). Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L3: This computation is used in the reachability updates. Connection to L5: It relies on basic addition. Common mistakes include: adding jump to the wrong index, or misunderstanding that jump is the maximum steps (so i + jump is the max index). Real code pattern: reachable = i + nums[i]. This sub-problem can be practiced by computing this for various inputs to build familiarity.

examples.trace: Compute i + jump = 0 + 2 = 2. So from index 0 with jump 2, you can reach up to index 2.
Compute i + jump = 3 + 1 = 4. So from index 3 with jump 1, you can reach up to index 4.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Basic Iteration
ladders.desc: Prerequisite concept of accessing array elements by index and looping through them.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept covers how to work with arrays: accessing elements using indices and iterating through all elements sequentially. The intuition is that an array is a collection of items where each item has a position (index) starting from 0. To solve the Jump Game, we need to examine each element nums[i] where i is the index. Basic iteration allows us to visit each position in order. The algorithm: 1. Given an array nums of length n. 2. For i from 0 to n-1: a. Access nums[i] (the jump value at index i). b. Perform operations (e.g., print or compute). 3. This ensures we process every element. Time complexity is O(n) for traversing all n elements. Space complexity is O(1) if no extra storage used. Connection to L4: Iteration provides the indices for addition operations. Connection to higher levels: This is the basic loop structure for the greedy approach. Common mistakes include: off-by-one errors (using i <= n instead of i < n), confusing index with value, or not handling empty arrays. Real code pattern: for i in range(len(nums)): current = nums[i]. This sub-problem is essential for beginners to master before tackling more complex algorithms.

examples.output: Iteration: i=0 value=2, i=1 value=3, i=2 value=1
examples.trace: Start: n=3. i=0: access nums[0] = 2. i=1: access nums[1] = 3. i=2: access nums[2] = 1. End of loop.
Start: n=1. i=0: access nums[0] = 0. End of loop.

testCases.expected: Iteration: i=0 value=1, i=1 value=2, i=2 value=3
Iteration: i=0 value=0, i=1 value=0, i=2 value=0, i=3 value=0
